questions.question: In direct speech, where are the exact words of the speaker placed?

options.A: Before the reporting verb
options.B: Within inverted commas
options.C: After the conjunction 'that'
options.D: In a separate paragraph

questions.explanation: According to the source, the exact words of the speaker are put within inverted commas in direct speech [1].

questions.question: Which of these is NOT a characteristic of direct speech?

options.A: The first word begins with a capital letter.
options.B: The quoted words are separated from the reporting verb by a comma.
options.C: The conjunction 'that' is always used.
options.D: Inverted commas are used.

questions.explanation: The conjunction 'that' is used in indirect speech, not direct speech [1, 2].

questions.question: What is the verb that precedes the quoted words in direct speech called?

options.A: Finite verb
options.B: Modal verb
options.C: Reporting verb
options.D: Auxiliary verb

questions.explanation: The verb that precedes the direct speech is called the 'reporting verb' [3].

questions.question: How does a reported sentence end, regardless of its original type?

options.A: With a question mark
options.B: With an exclamation mark
options.C: With a full stop
options.D: With a comma

questions.explanation: The reported speech ends with a full stop, no matter what type of sentence the direct speech was [2].

questions.question: When is there NO change in the tense of the verb in indirect speech?

options.A: When the speaker is famous.
options.B: When the speaker’s words remain true at the time of reporting.
options.C: When the reporting verb is in the past tense.
options.D: When the statement is a question.

questions.explanation: If the speaker's words remain true or unchanged at the time of reporting, there is no change in tense [4].

questions.question: Convert to indirect: Father said, 'The sun rises in the east.'

options.A: Father said that the sun rose in the east.
options.B: Father said that the sun rises in the east.
options.C: Father says that the sun rises in the east.
options.D: Father said the sun had risen in the east.

questions.explanation: Universal truths do not change tense in reported speech [5, 6].

questions.question: Which of these stays in the present simple in indirect speech?

options.A: Habitual actions
options.B: Unplanned future actions
options.C: Personal opinions from the past
options.D: Temporary actions

questions.explanation: The present tense is not changed into past if the direct speech is a habitual action [5].

questions.question: If the reporting verb is in the present tense, how does the verb in indirect speech change?

options.A: It changes to past tense.
options.B: It changes to past perfect.
options.C: It remains unchanged.
options.D: It becomes a to-infinitive.

questions.explanation: Verbs in indirect speech remain unchanged when the reporting verb is in the present tense [6].

questions.question: Convert the Samaj's comment (Activity 1): 'The show is brilliant.'

options.A: The Samaj reported that the show is brilliant.
options.B: The Samaj reported that the show was brilliant.
options.C: The Samaj reported the show brilliant.
options.D: The Samaj says the show was brilliant.

questions.explanation: Following the backshift rule for past reporting verbs in Activity 1 [7, 8].

questions.question: Report the Dharitri's comment: 'We can't have a better show.'

options.A: The Dharitri reported that they can't have a better show.
options.B: The Dharitri reported that we couldn't have a better show.
options.C: The Dharitri reported that they couldn't have a better show.
options.D: The Dharitri reports they couldn't have a better show.

questions.explanation: Pronoun 'we' changes to 'they' and 'can't' changes to 'couldn't' [8, 9].

questions.question: Rewrite (Activity 3): Rakhal said, 'I shall never forget my childhood days.'

options.A: Rakhal said that he should never forget his childhood days.
options.B: Rakhal said that he would never forget his childhood days.
options.C: Rakhal said he will never forget his childhood days.
options.D: Rakhal said he should never have forgotten his childhood days.

questions.explanation: 'Shall' in first person often becomes 'would' when referring to a future intent in third person [9, 10].

questions.question: Convert: The man said, 'The house is on fire.'

options.A: The man said that the house is on fire.
options.B: The man said that the house was on fire.
options.C: The man says that the house was on fire.
options.D: The man said that the house had been on fire.

questions.explanation: Standard backshift from present simple to past simple [10].

questions.question: Change to indirect: She says, 'I don't like sweets.'

options.A: She says that she didn't like sweets.
options.B: She says that she doesn't like sweets.
options.C: She said that she didn't like sweets.
options.D: She says she hasn't liked sweets.

questions.explanation: When the reporting verb is 'says' (present), the tense remains unchanged [10].

questions.question: Change to indirect: The guide said, 'Puri is famous for the temple of Lord Jagannath.'

options.A: The guide said that Puri was famous for the temple.
options.B: The guide said that Puri is famous for the temple.
options.C: The guide says that Puri is famous for the temple.
options.D: The guide said that Puri has been famous for the temple.

questions.explanation: Geographical truths or permanent facts do not change tense [11].

questions.question: How is 'now' changed in indirect speech?

options.A: today
options.B: then
options.C: at this time
options.D: before

questions.explanation: The source lists 'now' changing to 'then' or 'at that time' [12].

questions.question: What does 'yesterday' become in indirect speech?

options.A: tomorrow
options.B: the day before
options.C: the following day
options.D: that day

questions.explanation: The source states 'yesterday' changes to 'the day before' or 'the previous day' [12].

questions.question: What does 'tomorrow' become in indirect speech?

options.A: the previous day
options.B: the next day
options.C: that day
options.D: then

questions.explanation: The source states 'tomorrow' changes to 'the next day' or 'the following day' [12].

questions.question: Change to indirect: He said, 'I am going home.'

options.A: He said that he was going home.
options.B: He said that he is going home.
options.C: He said he has been going home.
options.D: He says he was going home.

questions.explanation: Present progressive changes to past progressive [13].

questions.question: Identify the change: 'I have done my homework,' I said.

options.A: I said that I have done my homework.
options.B: I said that I had done my homework.
options.C: I said that I did my homework.
options.D: I said that I was doing my homework.

questions.explanation: Present perfect changes to past perfect [13].

questions.question: Convert: The peon said, 'I posted the letter.'

options.A: The peon said that he posted the letter.
options.B: The peon said that he had posted the letter.
options.C: The peon said that he has posted the letter.
options.D: The peon says he had posted the letter.

questions.explanation: Past simple changes to past perfect if the action happened before the time of reporting [14].

questions.question: Does the past simple change if it refers to a historical fact? 'India became independent on 15th August 1947,' the teacher said.

options.A: Yes: had become independent
options.B: No: became independent

questions.explanation: Historical facts relevant at the time of reporting stay in the past simple [15].

questions.question: Convert: She said, 'When I left home, it began to rain.'

options.A: She said that when she had left home, it had begun to rain.
options.B: She said that when she left home, it began to rain.
options.C: She said when she was leaving, it began to rain.
options.D: She said that when she left home, it was beginning to rain.

questions.explanation: In time clauses where two actions occurred simultaneously, the past simple remains unchanged [16].

questions.question: True or False: Past perfect and past perfect progressive forms do not change in indirect speech.

options.A: True
options.B: False

questions.explanation: The source explicitly states these forms do not change [17].

questions.question: How does the modal 'can' change in indirect speech?

options.A: could
options.B: would
options.C: can't
options.D: must

questions.explanation: The source lists 'can' changing to 'could' [18].

questions.question: What does 'may' change to in indirect speech?

options.A: might
options.B: could
options.C: maybe
options.D: must

questions.explanation: The source lists 'may' changing to 'might' [18].

questions.question: When 'must' refers to the future, it changes into:

options.A: must
options.B: had to
options.C: would have to
options.D: should have to

questions.explanation: When must refers to the future, it changes to 'would have to' [18].

questions.question: When reporting a question, what is NOT used after the reporting verb?

options.A: the wh-word
options.B: the conjunction 'that'
options.C: if
options.D: whether

questions.explanation: The conjunction 'that' is not used after the reporting verb in questions [19, 20].

questions.question: Report: Rakesh said to his servant, 'Where are you going?'

options.A: Rakesh asked his servant where he was going.
options.B: Rakesh asked his servant where are you going.
options.C: Rakesh asked his servant that where he was going.
options.D: Rakesh asked his servant where he had gone.

questions.explanation: Wh-questions become statements beginning with the wh-word [19, 21].

questions.question: Report: He said to me, 'Do you know him?'

options.A: He asked me if I know him.
options.B: He asked me if I knew him.
options.C: He told me that I knew him.
options.D: He asked me that if I knew him.

questions.explanation: Yes-No questions become statements beginning with 'if' or 'whether' [20, 22].

questions.question: In imperatives, 'say' is replaced by which verbs?

options.A: tell, order, request
options.B: advise, remind, warn
options.C: suggest, command, beg
options.D: All of these

questions.explanation: The source lists all these verbs as replacements for 'say' in imperatives [23].

questions.question: What is used before the verb of the imperative in indirect speech?

options.A: that
options.B: to / not to
options.C: if / whether
options.D: because

questions.explanation: Imperatives take the 'to + infinitive' form [23].

questions.question: Report: Mother said to me, 'Don't go to bed late.'

options.A: Mother advised me that I don't go to bed late.
options.B: Mother advised me not to go to bed late.
options.C: Mother advised me to not go to bed late.
options.D: Mother advised me that don't go to bed late.

questions.explanation: Negative imperatives use 'not to' before the verb [23, 24].

questions.question: Report an exclamation: He said, 'What a splendid sunset this is!'

options.A: He said that it was a splendid sunset.
options.B: He exclaimed in joy that it was really a splendid sunset.
options.C: He asked what a splendid sunset that was.
options.D: He said what a splendid sunset.

questions.explanation: Exclamatory sentences become statements, often using verbs like 'exclaimed in joy/sorrow' [25, 26].

questions.question: How is 'Liar!' reported in indirect speech?

options.A: He said that he was a liar.
options.B: He called him a liar.
options.C: He exclaimed that he is a liar.
options.D: He told a liar.

questions.explanation: The source shows 'Liar!' being changed to 'He called me a liar' [27].

questions.question: Report a greeting: He said, 'Hello! Where do you live?'

options.A: He said hello and where I lived.
options.B: He greeted me and asked where I lived.
options.C: He said hello that where I lived.
options.D: He greeted me that where do I live.

questions.explanation: 'Hello' is reported as 'greeted' [27].

questions.question: Report: 'Can you work out this sum?' the teacher said to me.

options.A: The teacher asked if I can work out the sum.
options.B: The teacher asked if I could work out that sum.
options.C: The teacher asked if you could work out that sum.
options.D: The teacher asked me that if I could work out that sum.

questions.explanation: 'Can' changes to 'could' and 'this' changes to 'that' [27].

questions.question: Report (Activity 7): 'Consult a doctor immediately,' he said to me.

options.A: He said to consult a doctor.
options.B: He advised me to consult a doctor immediately.
options.C: He advised me that I should consult a doctor.
options.D: He advised me consult a doctor.

questions.explanation: Imperative of advice using 'advised' and 'to + verb' [28].

questions.question: Report: The captain said, 'Hurrah! We have scored two goals!'

options.A: The captain exclaimed in joy that they had scored two goals.
options.B: The captain said hurrah they had scored two goals.
options.C: The captain exclaimed that we have scored two goals.
options.D: The captain said they scored two goals.

questions.explanation: 'Hurrah' shows joy; present perfect 'have scored' changes to past perfect 'had scored' [28].

questions.question: Report (Activity 8): 'Are you leaving today?' Maggie asked.

options.A: Maggie asked if I was leaving today.
options.B: Maggie asked if I was leaving that day.
options.C: Maggie asked if you were leaving that day.
options.D: Maggie asked whether I am leaving that day.

questions.explanation: 'Today' changes to 'that day' and present progressive to past progressive [29].

questions.question: Which word is used to emphasize the intensity of an exclamation in indirect speech?

options.A: much
options.B: very
options.C: too
options.D: so

questions.explanation: The source states we use 'very' to emphasize intensity [26].

questions.question: What does 'this week' change to in indirect speech?

options.A: that week
options.B: next week
options.C: the previous week
options.D: last week

questions.explanation: 'this week' changes to 'that week' [12, 30].

questions.question: How does 'here' change in indirect speech?

options.A: now
options.B: there
options.C: this place
options.D: where

questions.explanation: The source lists 'here' changing to 'there' [12].

questions.question: When 'must' is used in the meaning of certainty, does it change?

options.A: Yes: becomes 'had to'
options.B: No: remains 'must'

questions.explanation: When must is used in the meaning of certainty, it remains unchanged [18].

questions.question: Sample Question: He said to me, 'Did you know him?'

options.A: He told me if I know him.
options.B: He asked me if I knew him.
options.C: He asked me if I had known him.
options.D: He asked me if I have known him.

questions.explanation: Model Paper question 47 [31, 32].

questions.question: Report: 'I go for a walk every morning,' a scout said.

options.A: A scout said that he went for a walk every morning.
options.B: A scout said that he goes for a walk every morning.
options.C: A scout said that he would go for a walk every morning.
options.D: A scout told that he will go for a walk every morning.

questions.explanation: Habitual actions remain in the present simple in indirect speech [33, 34].

questions.question: Change: 'Good evening,' he said.

options.A: He said me good evening.
options.B: He asked me good evening.
options.C: He wished a good evening.
options.D: He wished me a good evening.

questions.explanation: Model Paper question 44 [35, 36].

questions.question: In reported speech, what happens to the inverted commas?

options.A: They are doubled.
options.B: They are omitted.
options.C: They are moved to the end.
options.D: They stay the same.

questions.explanation: In indirect speech, the inverted commas are omitted [2].

questions.question: How is 'ago' changed in indirect speech?

options.A: before
options.B: since
options.C: then
options.D: yesterday

questions.explanation: The source lists 'ago' changing to 'before' [12].

questions.question: What is the structure for reporting a Yes-No question?

options.A: subject + ask + that + statement
options.B: subject + ask + if/whether + statement
options.C: subject + ask + what + statement
options.D: subject + tell + if/whether + statement

questions.explanation: Yes-No questions become statements beginning with 'if' or 'whether' [20].

questions.question: Report: 'Let's go on a picnic,' she said.

options.A: She said to go on a picnic.
options.B: She suggested going on a picnic.
options.C: She told to go on a picnic.
options.D: She said that let's go on a picnic.

questions.explanation: 'Let's' indicates a suggestion and is reported with 'suggested + -ing' or 'suggested that... should' [37].

questions.question: How is 'this week' changed when used as a time expression?

options.A: that week
options.B: the week before
options.C: the next week
options.D: this week

questions.explanation: 'this week' changes to 'that week' [30].

questions.question: If something is said in continuation of another statement, which verb is used?

options.A: ask
options.B: add
options.C: reply
options.D: deny

questions.explanation: We generally use 'add', 'continue', or 'further said' for continuations [38].
Questions in reported speech are transformed into statements [19].

options.A: Interrogative sentences
options.B: Declarative sentences (statements)
options.C: Imperative sentences
options.D: Exclamatory sentences

questions.question: Questions in indirect speech take the form of:
Change into indirect speech: Rabi said to me, "I know where you were born."

options.A: Rabi told me that he knew where I was born.
options.B: Rabi asked me where I was born.
options.C: Rabi told me that I knew where he was born.
options.D: Rabi said to me that he knows where I was born.

questions.explanation: In indirect speech, the present simple 'know' and 'were born' backshift to past simple and past perfect, respectively, and pronouns change to match the reporter.

questions.question: Change into indirect speech: Father said, "Morning is at six today."

options.A: Father said that morning is at six today.
options.B: Father said that morning was at six that day.
options.C: Father said that morning had been at six that day.
options.D: Father says that morning was at six today.

questions.explanation: The present simple 'is' changes to 'was', and the adverbial 'today' changes to 'that day'.

questions.question: Change into indirect speech: She said, "I’ll help you in the hour of your need."

options.A: She said that she would help me in the hour of my need.
options.B: She said that I would help her in the hour of her need.
options.C: She said she will help me in the hour of my need.
options.D: She told she should help me in the hour of my need.

questions.explanation: The modal 'will' changes to 'would', and pronouns 'I' and 'you' shift to 'she' and 'me'.

questions.question: Change into indirect speech: He said, "I collected these pebbles at the seashore."

options.A: He said that he collected those pebbles at the seashore.
options.B: He said that he had collected those pebbles at the seashore.
options.C: He said that he had collected these pebbles at the seashore.
options.D: He told he was collecting those pebbles at the seashore.

questions.explanation: Past simple 'collected' changes to past perfect 'had collected', and the demonstrative 'these' changes to 'those'.

questions.question: Change into indirect speech: I said to him, "You didn’t keep fit last year."

options.A: I told him that he didn't keep fit the previous year.
options.B: I told him that he hadn't kept fit the previous year.
options.C: I told him that you hadn't kept fit last year.
options.D: I asked him if he hadn't kept fit last year.

questions.explanation: The past simple 'didn't keep' changes to past perfect 'hadn't kept', and 'last year' becomes 'the previous year'.

questions.question: Change into indirect speech: The teacher said to me, "You must work hard for your examination."

options.A: The teacher told me that I must work hard for my examination.
options.B: The teacher told me that I had to work hard for my examination.
options.C: Both A and B are correct.
options.D: The teacher told me that you must work hard for your examination.

questions.explanation: In indirect speech, 'must' can remain 'must' or change to 'had to' depending on the sense of obligation.

questions.question: Change into indirect speech: The doctor said to me, "You mustn’t smoke."

options.A: The doctor told me that I didn't have to smoke.
options.B: The doctor told me that I mustn't smoke.
options.C: The doctor advised me to smoke.
options.D: The doctor said I wouldn't smoke.

questions.explanation: The source specifies that 'mustn't' generally remains 'mustn't' in indirect speech.

questions.question: Change into indirect speech: Sushil said, "We celebrate our Republic Day on 26th January every year."

options.A: Sushil said that they celebrated their Republic Day on 26th January every year.
options.B: Sushil said that they celebrate their Republic Day on 26th January every year.
options.C: Sushil said that we celebrate our Republic Day on 26th January every year.
options.D: Sushil asked if they celebrate Republic Day every year.

questions.explanation: For habitual or repeated actions that remain true, the present simple tense remains unchanged.

questions.question: Change into indirect speech: He said, "I was playing the violin every morning last year."

options.A: He said that he was playing the violin every morning the previous year.
options.B: He said that he had been playing the violin every morning the previous year.
options.C: He said he played the violin every morning last year.
options.D: He told he had played the violin every morning the previous year.

questions.explanation: Past progressive 'was playing' is usually changed into past perfect progressive 'had been playing'.

questions.question: Change into indirect speech: He said, "These are my toys."

options.A: He said that these were his toys.
options.B: He said that those were his toys.
options.C: He said that they were his toys.
options.D: Both B and C are possible.

questions.explanation: When 'these' is used as a pronoun, it can become 'those' or 'they' in indirect speech.

questions.question: Change into indirect speech: My friend said to me, "When did you return home last night?"

options.A: My friend asked me when I had returned home the previous night.
options.B: My friend asked me when did I return home last night.
options.C: My friend told me when I had returned home the previous night.
options.D: My friend asked when I returned home last night.

questions.explanation: In reporting a wh-question, the question becomes a statement, past simple 'did return' becomes past perfect 'had returned', and 'last night' becomes 'the previous night'.

questions.question: Change into indirect speech: Gita said to her younger brother, "Who are you talking to on the phone?"

options.A: Gita asked her younger brother who he was talking to on the phone.
options.B: Gita asked her younger brother who was he talking to on the phone.
options.C: Gita asked her younger brother that who he was talking to.
options.D: Gita wanted to know who her brother is talking to.

questions.explanation: The wh-question 'Who are you talking to' becomes a statement 'who he was talking to', with tense backshifted from present progressive to past progressive.

questions.question: Change into indirect speech: The teacher said to me, "Have you done your homework?"

options.A: The teacher asked me that if I had done my homework.
options.B: The teacher asked me if I had done my homework.
options.C: The teacher asked me if I have done my homework.
options.D: The teacher asked me whether had I done my homework.

questions.explanation: Yes-No questions are reported using 'if' or 'whether' followed by a statement, and present perfect shifts to past perfect.

questions.question: Change into indirect speech: "Was it raining heavily at your place last month?" he said to me.

options.A: He asked me if it was raining heavily at my place last month.
options.B: He asked me if it had been raining heavily at my place the previous month.
options.C: He asked me if it rained heavily at my place the month before.
options.D: He asked me whether it was raining heavily at your place the month before.

questions.explanation: Past progressive 'was it raining' backshifts to 'had been raining', and 'last month' changes to 'the previous month'.

questions.question: Report the question (Activity 5): Mahesh asked, "What is the name of your sister's husband?"

options.A: Mahesh asked what the name of my sister's husband was.
options.B: Mahesh asked what was the name of my sister's husband.
options.C: Mahesh asked me what is the name of my sister's husband.
options.D: Mahesh wanted to know the name of my sister husband.

questions.explanation: The interrogative form is changed into a declarative statement with backshift.

questions.question: Report the question (Activity 5): Mahesh asked, "Has he ever been abroad?"

options.A: Mahesh asked if he has ever been abroad.
options.B: Mahesh asked if he had ever been abroad.
options.C: Mahesh asked whether has he ever been abroad.
options.D: Mahesh asked that if he had ever been abroad.

questions.explanation: Present perfect 'has... been' changes to past perfect 'had... been' in a reported question.

questions.question: Report the question (Activity 5): Mahesh asked, "What salary does he get?"

options.A: Mahesh asked what salary did he get.
options.B: Mahesh asked what salary he got.
options.C: Mahesh asked what salary he gets.
options.D: Mahesh asked what salary does he get.

questions.explanation: The present simple 'does... get' becomes past simple 'he got' in the reported statement.

questions.question: Change into indirect speech: The teacher said to the students, "Move out of the class silently."

options.A: The teacher told the students to move out of the class silently.
options.B: The teacher told the students that move out of the class silently.
options.C: The teacher asked the students to moved out of the class silently.
options.D: The teacher ordered that the students move out silently.

questions.explanation: Imperative sentences are reported using the structure: reporting verb + object + to-infinitive.

questions.question: Change into indirect speech: "Please help me cross the road," he said to the policeman.

options.A: He said the policeman to help him cross the road.
options.B: He requested the policeman to help him cross the road.
options.C: He requested the policeman that he should help him cross the road.
options.D: He asked the policeman to please help him.

questions.explanation: The word 'please' indicates a request, which is reported using 'requested' followed by the to-infinitive.

questions.question: Change into indirect speech: She said, "Let’s go on a picnic to Nandankanan tomorrow."

options.A: She said that they should go on a picnic to Nandankanan tomorrow.
options.B: She suggested their going on a picnic to Nandankanan the next day.
options.C: She suggested that they should go on a picnic to Nandankanan the next day.
options.D: Both B and C are correct.

questions.explanation: Suggestions with 'Let's' can be reported using 'suggested + -ing' or 'suggested that... should'.

questions.question: Change into indirect speech: My sister said, "Remember to bring the umbrella."

options.A: My sister told me to remember to bring the umbrella.
options.B: My sister reminded me to bring the umbrella.
options.C: My sister said that I remember to bring the umbrella.
options.D: Both A and B are acceptable.

questions.explanation: The imperative of reminder can be reported with 'told... to remember' or the specific verb 'reminded'.

questions.question: Change into indirect speech: He said, "Good luck!"

options.A: He said good luck.
options.B: He wished me good luck.
options.C: He told me good luck.
options.D: He exclaimed good luck.

questions.explanation: Wishes and greetings are reported with the verb 'wished'.

questions.question: Change into indirect speech: He said, "How dreadful!"

options.A: He said that it was how dreadful.
options.B: He exclaimed that it was very dreadful.
options.C: He said that it is very dreadful.
options.D: He exclaimed in joy it was dreadful.

questions.explanation: Exclamations are converted into statements using 'exclaimed' and intensity markers like 'very'.

questions.question: Change into indirect speech: He said, "Good morning!"

options.A: He said me good morning.
options.B: He wished me a good morning.
options.C: He greeted me good morning.
options.D: He told me a good morning.

questions.explanation: Greetings like 'Good morning' are reported using the verb 'wished'.

questions.question: Change into indirect speech (Activity 7): "Don’t get addicted to tea," he said to me.

options.A: He advised me not to get addicted to tea.
options.B: He said me not to get addicted to tea.
options.C: He warned me to not get addicted to tea.
options.D: He advised that I don't get addicted to tea.

questions.explanation: Negative imperatives of advice are reported with 'advised... not to'.

questions.question: Change into indirect speech (Activity 7): "Your mouth smells foul. Don’t open it," he said.

options.A: He said that my mouth smelled foul and told me not to open it.
options.B: He said that your mouth smelled foul and asked not to open it.
options.C: He said that his mouth smelled foul and told me to not open it.
options.D: He told me my mouth smells foul and don't open it.

questions.explanation: This combined sentence requires backshift for the statement and 'not to' for the imperative.

questions.question: Change into indirect speech (Activity 7): The General said, "Bravo! You have done wonders!"

options.A: The General said bravo that they had done wonders.
options.B: The General applauded them, saying that they had done wonders.
options.C: The General told they had done wonders.
options.D: The General exclaimed they have done wonders.

questions.explanation: 'Bravo' indicates applause or praise, often reported with the verb 'applauded'.

questions.question: Change into indirect speech (Activity 7): The teacher said to Nirmal, "What a lazy boy you are!"

options.A: The teacher told Nirmal that he was a very lazy boy.
options.B: The teacher exclaimed that Nirmal was a very lazy boy.
options.C: Both A and B are correct.
options.D: The teacher asked Nirmal if he was a lazy boy.

questions.explanation: Exclamatory sentences can be reported as statements using backshift and 'very' for intensity.

questions.question: Change into indirect speech (Activity 7): My friend said to me, "How nice of you to have come!"

options.A: My friend exclaimed that it was very nice of me to have come.
options.B: My friend said it was nice of you to have come.
options.C: My friend thanked me for coming.
options.D: My friend told me how nice it was.

questions.explanation: Standard exclamation to statement conversion.

questions.question: Change into indirect speech (Activity 7): "If only I had the wings of a dove!" he said.

options.A: He wished he had the wings of a dove.
options.B: He said if he had the wings of a dove.
options.C: He exclaimed that he had the wings of a dove.
options.D: He asked if he had wings.

questions.explanation: The phrase 'If only I had' indicates a wish, reported using the verb 'wished'.
